speaker: interviewer
text: August 27th, 1995. The survivor's name is Henry Rosmarin. This is a walking interview in Sachsenhausen, Germany. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Rosmarin. I'm a survivor. We're here today at the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And give him the sign, again. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Rosmarin. I'm a survivor of the Holocaust. This is August the 27th, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just walked through the gate which says, Arbeit Macht Frei, a famous slogan of the Nazis. Oppressors who want us to believe in a farce of Arbeit Macht Frei, which means work liberates. They camp we are in, the concentration camp we are in now, is Sachsenhausen, which is located about 50 miles-- 50 kilometers, rather-- north of Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Camp Sachsenhausen is the famous-- infamous, rather-- for its atrocities like most of the camps. Except this one has a personal meaning to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although I wasn't here personally, my brother and I-- who were the youngest survivors of our family. As we were separated in 1943, he wound up [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Camp Sachsenhausen. And how he came to this camp is perhaps a story that needs to be told. And the reason I'm telling this story, because my brother is not alive anymore, unfortunately. And I would like to-- to-- adds to his experience because he is not-- speak of his experiences, because unfortunately he is not here to speak for himself. His survival was rather unique. Like most of us, each and every one of us survivors has a story to tell. And each story [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is short of a miracle perhaps. Somewhat short of a miracle. Perhaps a miracle in itself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I won't speak of my own survival, but I would like to focus pretty well on my brother's survival and along with a group of young boys which were picked in a camp of Auschwitz to come here to this godforsaken land. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before I begin the survival story, I would like to more or less point out the location with which we are in. As I mentioned before, we are 50 miles north of Berlin. The Camp is Sachsenhausen, S-A-S-H-S-E-N-H-A-U-S-E-N. I just came through the main gate with its famous Arbeit Macht Frei-- work liberates. The gate usually is occupied by-- the upstairs and the downstairs, of course, and with a tower in the middle. That's when usually the higher-- the higher officers in charge of the camp had their offices overlooking the marching in and marching out of the prisoners. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And camp was started, I believe, in-- I remember I was a young boy still in Poland, and I heard my father talk about Sachsenhausen, Buchenwald, Gross-Rosen, and Dachau. Those were names that still ring in my ears because I heard them first from my parents. Little did I know when this camp was started and I was-- let's see, 1936-- I was like a 10-year-old boy. Little did I know that someday both my brother and I will be in the before-mentioned camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The camp at first was occupied by political prisoners, free democrats, communists, or anyone who Hitler-- who Hitler viewed as an enemy of the state. Among them were probably German nationals, for the most part. And later on as Hitler's armies began to conquer the rest of Europe, the camp grew from its original perhaps 5,000 to about 25,000. And in 1943, it swelled into 50,000 and 60,000. By that time, Jewish prisoners were coming in, ones who were picked through Auschwitz, or Gross-Rosen, or Buchenwald and were housed under horrible conditions here. The towers are not the visible from here. However, at back of this gate-- which we'll show later-- there still remains the base of the crematorium, mass graves, and a few barracks are still left from its original days. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We're inside the camp grounds, the concentration camp grounds, right now. Having walked through the-- just walked through to this famous Arbeit Macht Frei gate. In its original fashion, in its original days, here is where they-- what they used to call [NON-ENGLISH], which means the formation of prisoners would march through. In some camps and in some instances, even singing. Nazi is singing German marches. And always, by all means, marching to the beat and in step. And god forbid, one should make a misstep, or perhaps fall down, or miss the step by not listening to the kapo who would give the eine, zwei, drei, vier. [NON-ENGLISH] zwei, drei, vier. And if one would have his mind on his liter of-- a liter is one quart of a gallon of this watery soup which is about to be handed out. And perhaps missed a step or missed the beat of this that the kapo would be shouting. And if once he made a misstep, he would be taken out of the formation, and whipped and beaten, and sometimes even beaten to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So having walked through here, I can just see and hear the sounds of the goose steps, except these goose steps have no officer's boots. These were just shoes, wooden shoes, of inmates like myself, and stepping to the rhythm, and hoping to get there as quick as possible to get to the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks from here-- this is called what they called the appellplatz. Appell means a roll call. Once the columns of prisoners would walk through here, they would then form in formation by barracks. Barracks so-and-so and so-and-so would stay in formation-- again, in five, to be counted. And of course, in those days, that's before calculators and computers. Each [NON-ENGLISH], or one in charge of that particular column or group of people, would know that he has 120 or 130 people to be accounted for. And he would begin his counting. After which, he would submit them to the main, to another person who would then total it up. And he would submit it to a person whose barracks would be assembled way beyond the-- which is now a wall. And all of this would be tallied up. This was a process of perhaps anywhere from a half an hour to probably an hour and a half. And woe to the prisoner who was hidden and perhaps couldn't be counted. He again would be taken out and beaten. As we stood here being counted, and hoping that this process would be over very quickly so that we can finally get to the barrack and get in line to see if this daily ration of this watery soup, and perhaps find maybe a potato swimming in it, and the 300 grams of bread. 300 grams equals-- in American bread would be perhaps-- of a loaf of bread, if you divide a loaf of bread in five. We'd say 1/5 of a loaf or say a 1 1/2 pound of bread. And after the columns marched in and were counted, and the count was correct-- and of course, these Nazi beasts would be standing here with their boots shiny and with their riding crops hitting the boots just waiting. And the dogs, the German shepherds, by their side just waiting for-- for-- for someone to jump on, to pounce on. And for any small infractions such as not standing straight at attention. And of course, the higher officers would look through the windows and they delighted in the sight which they have just witnessed, and enjoyed their mastery over these-- these emaciated bodies and waves of human beings. Here we are in front of the barracks, which represents the typical units of housing. Barracks were divided into four rooms. In German, called [NON-ENGLISH]. Each room housed somewhere between 25 and 30 inmates. The beds were bunks, usually three-tier high. Myself, a little guy, would probably sleep-- they put me up high, so it'd be easier for me to get up. The barracks, each [NON-ENGLISH] had a-- the Germans believed in authority. They would appoint what they call a [NON-ENGLISH] elders there, which means the elderly of that particular room and that barrack. He was responsible for the cleanliness. He was responsible for the division of food, if you can call it that. And that was always when-- when the soup and the bread were handed out. The soup, of course, was handed out-- distributed, if you will-- by a ladle-- a ladle and, of course, a kettle. A kettle would hold, perhaps, somewhere-- I'm going to guess maybe a 5 gallon kettle. And the orderly in charge of the soup would have a constant job to be reminded by the prisoners who were standing in line anxiously waiting to see this little hot water with perhaps a rotten potato swimming in it or maybe a noodle or two. And they would always position themselves-- including myself-- I was always hoping I'd get towards the end because the heavy stuff, the potatoes, would be found, perhaps, on the bottom.

speaker: interviewer
text: If this kapo who was standing-- and a kapo incidentally, is also a prisoner, an elderly prisoner. Kapo means [NON-ENGLISH]. He would supervise-- he would just stand by the side and watch the orderly to hand out the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the soup. And if someone would murmur under his breath something like, why don't you stir it a little faster? He would take the ladle from the-- the orderly and hit him over the head and perhaps split his head open.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could not change position once in line. And when you came in line and came to the front of the kettle, you would want to get the soup and the piece of bread handed and made quickly to the corner of this room or [NON-ENGLISH]. Close to his little bunk where one would quickly sit down, and eat the soup, and perhaps dunk the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'm sorry I lost my composure. Because as I'm trying to describe this scene, I was suddenly overcome by certain scenes. And I could see the eyes of my fellow inmates who would anxiously look, would envy at the other inmate because he already-- because, he, himself, finished his soup, and took his bread, and wiped off the bottom of that little aluminum can or the dish. And the other one was perhaps chewing slow, didn't have any teeth. And here was still some bread left. And they were looking, staring at him. And I must apologize for-- for my composure because I can see these guys in front of my eyes now. And after this was distributed, you wanted to make a choice-- either eat that bread now or maybe save for later. Later in the morning, if-- if you could make it through the night hiding your bread under the sack of straw, which was supposed to substitute for a mattress. Or hide it under your shirt. Or perhaps eat it and be safe. Just eat it. Just consume it so no one would steal it. Because yes, there were incidents of stealing bread, or robbing, or even by sheer force taking the bread from somebody. And I'm sure it's hard to imagine one human being doing this to another. But I think humans can be also like animals. They would fight over a prey. At any rate, after-- after this was consumed, and it was counted again. The prisoners were counted again. And announced, of course, there in 15 minutes would be lichter aus, which means lights were to be turned out. And you can finally hop into your bunk and spread your weary bones. And perhaps dry out your clothes. Awaiting maybe a good night's sleep. It wasn't really a long night, because they would wake us at 4:30 or 5:00 in the morning. But this at least was one day accomplished. We lived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We lived through a day-- another day marked off in a mental calendar. How many days were left no one knew. But I know, I myself felt a sense of victory. I've survived one more day. And of course, as I lay on the bunk, I was contemplating what tomorrow will bring. Maybe another day. Maybe I'll get a little better job. Maybe with my luck, I'll be in line for the butter more than soup. Or maybe my slice of bread will be a little larger. And so this was the life in these barracks. If I may point out for a minute the left and the right. Everything had to be scrubbed. The beds had to be made. When the whistle blew in the morning, everything had to be cleaned up. And another day would begin. I would like to point out to this barrack, particularly R1. Which is a-- R stands for [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] stands for sick bay, which is an euphemism for getting people well. Now, this particular barrack housed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 11 young boys whose story I would like to relate now. 11 young boys who were marked for the most inhuman treatment that humanity has ever heard of. I know I live in California now. And I know we have animal groups who are vividly protesting experiments on cats, dogs, mice. And I can sympathize with them. However, experiments are probably necessary. But these young boys were picked to be human guinea pigs. The Germans called them [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH]. Meaning if a kaninchen is a rabbit, a little rabbit, these were-- these were human guinea pigs. And Germans called, again, a rabbit rather than a pig. And experiments were-- they went under-- a series of medical experiments were performed on them on the most horrible conditions, as we will see in a few moments. I know my brother, Max-- may his soul rest in peace-- he was injected with the virus of yellow jaundice. Yellow jaundice, typhoid, and malaria. The latter one, malaria, was experimented vividly in 1944-- end of 1943 and '44-- because General Rommel's troops in Africa were contracting malaria fever. And they were-- they were really escalating the process of finding a cure for the virus. And this was performed in-- in the barracks such as this. This is one of them here, which we'll go in in a moment and see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's called pathologie. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were-- besides the experiments, there were also other atrocities performed on human bodies. Human parts, skin graftings, skin-- saving your skin for various-- for purposes of lamp shades, what other, what have you. But we'll now enter the inside and see the gruesome-- the gruesome tools and the works of the Nazi beasts. This is probably hard to believe for the average person viewing this. But it did happen. And five of the original 11 are still alive. And they recently gathered here for a-- a gathering commemorating the 50th year of liberation. So let us now enter to the inside. And I'll continue with the story of human experiments. Yes. We are now entering the barrack, which was called the pathologie. This is where the unfortunate, horrible acts took place. Where they cut up human bodies for experiments and did all kinds of unheard of things. Such as skulls, saving of skulls, saving of human skin. Experimenting as to what practical applications this may have, importance in medicine-- which was probably very little. But the important thing is the torture of a human being. If we may step in the other room, there's a picture of perhaps one of the-- one of the biggest murderers in this particular camp of Sachsenhausen. We also have-- I must also mention, people who are visiting here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people who want to come to see the former camp, students perhaps of history, or ordinary German people who are perhaps-- want to see for themselves what their forefathers did and if it's possible that such a thing could have happened. Dr. Viktor Lewe was an SS untersturmfuhrer, equivalent to perhaps an American-- probably something like a captain. He, needless to say, was a sadistic human being. The experiments, which I am familiar with-- the one that my brother was subjected to-- were initiated by Dr. Mengele back in perhaps early '40s-- '40 or '41. My brother's odyssey into this camp began when the transport was unloading Jews in Auschwitz in 1943 marked for a certain death. As he was about to jump out of the boxcars, among the shouts of [GERMAN]. And of course, shouts were ringing out. The dogs were barking. Riding crops were landing on the heads and necks of the inmates-- Jewish men, women, and children. My brother had the presence of mind-- he noticed in front of the boxcars a group of officers with the insignia of medicine-- with the snake of the medicine insignia on the lapels-- pointing to some young boys segregated to the left and right. He jumped out in front of one of the officers, stood at attention, and exclaiming, I volunteer. I'm in good health. I can work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The officer looked at him. First, he hit him with his riding crop and told him something, [NON-ENGLISH], which means you really are very fresh. You have a lot of guts. Who are you addressing to? The other officer then turned to him and asked him pointed questions such as, have you had the measles? Have you ever had high malaria fever? Or you have typhoid fever? He says, no, no, no. I'm healthy, I'm healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before he knew it, he was segregated with a group of 20 young men. And they were quickly herded, taken off from the siding where the train was emptying, where the boxcars were emptying. And he found himself in another barrack. Out of the 20, they made seven to the showers, and issued them right away prison garbs, striped uniforms. And another selection begun. They found out they only need about 10 or 11. He was handed a number, I believe of-- he told me of 18 or 19. And he overheard the officer say, we won't need-- we won't need that many. And he was only hoping that he will-- whatever this selection is for-- that he can make it. There was another number dropped on the floor. He quickly picked it up and hid it under his arm. And as they were calling the numbers from 1 to 12, he used this lower number, which was either 9 or 11, something like that. And he made it into that group. And still not knowing what this is all for. Towards the evening, they were issued [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some more underwear and separated in a separate barracks and asked not to talk to, not to communicate with anyone. The next day, a doctor came. And my brother, of course, recognized Dr. Mengele along with two other [INAUDIBLE]. And they start asking pointed questions about sickness and whatever. And told them not to worry, that they will be shipped to another camp, and they will be well taken care of. And only through their cooperation-- again, the Arbeit Macht Frei, you will survive. Nothing will happen to you. You will work for the German Reich. Then a few days later, they were shipped out and came to this Camp Sachsenhausen. The barracks, which I pointed out a while ago, the one on the left-hand side, was their permanent home, if you will.
